item.title: Humans Are Only So Much Theory
item.description:
I’ll be the first to admit, I often get very lost in the world of academia and academic literature.

Not because I find these articles and essays particularly challenging or difficult to read (though they most certainly are), but because I very frequently find myself feeling very detached from what it is I am reading. Especially when it comes to the social sciences. I’ll explain what I mean.

Currently, my entire college schedule consists purely of four social sciences and humanities courses: a course in anthropology, a course in economics, a course philosophy, and a course in English. During the semester, my actual course work consists mostly of reading other scholars’ work, being told to remember some philosopher’s notion about the way human beings are or a poet’s genius hidden within his words.

item.title: Alysa Liu and the Purely Value-Driven Mentality of Modern Sports
item.description:
If you haven’t heard already, Alysa Liu, the 20-year-old female figure skater from Oakland, California, recently won gold for Team USA at the 2026 Winter Olympics by scoring 226.79 points! Woooooo! USA, USA, USA!

But, wait a minute, if all that happened was that Alysa Liu won Team USA a gold medal at the Olympics and, at least for a brief moment, united our nation’s vast partisan differences through her winning of the first female figure skating gold medal in nearly 24 years for the United States, why the hell am I even writing an article about this? Isn’t this topic already oversaturated enough?

item.title: It SHOULD Be Those "Damn Phones"
item.description:
Conceptualizing other human beings is fucking hard, man.

Don’t get me wrong, I can acknowledge that other people on this planet exist. It’s not a mystery to me that there exists other meat blobs walking around with a mind of their own (this is not a post on solipsism, in case you were wondering).

Yet, despite my experience and knowledge of the intricate lives of many of the closest individuals to me, the one task that seemingly is the most difficult to grasp is developing an actual empathy for strangers.
